item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
According to C-Dives

"knowledge.a.jpg" and "knowledge.d.png" contain a NC material.

https://www.flickr.com/photos/squirrel_brand/4201670980

"knowledge.b.jpg" is 403 now, but it was CC BY 2.0 for sure; I checked last year.

As such, both images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really disagree that this would be 'very confusing for most', and that's thanks to that very helpful infographic tucked away in that footnote that lays the whole thing out very cleanly. I would use those myself if I had the patience to learn how to make them, because in this case it let me sidestep the issue of keeping track of what exactly everything's relationship was to everything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here and there; using basic perspective of an average Joe member of the site, this SCP would be very confusing for most. None the less, the smaller details are what I like most about this SCP. It really shows its uniqueness that differs itself from the other SCP's, and gives off a genuine feel.

Good work; upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how the guy looks at the Foundation. Great article, effective pictures, interesting excerpts, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even more than the idea of a magical taxi service running through the secret under-roads and shadowy passageways of old London town, I love the idea of this being the remnants of an SCP that ended not through a terrible conflict that left nothing but echoes or madness run amok or an ancient horror lurking and ready to strike once more, but simply because the well-meaning people behind it decided that it was time to go out on a high note and that someone else might get some small pleasure out of what they'd done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me a great deal of Neil Gaiman's Neverwhere (and that's a good thing). Have your upvote, madam.

As for people who like/dislike the internal Foundation documents…I'd say it's a pretty subjective topic. I personally like it, as it gives the Foundation a more realistic feel, as actual scientific documentation cross-references different sources constantly, and the Foundation would surely have some sort of forum for the publication of research on scips that aren't super top secret classified. But if you don't like it, you don't. I don't like sloppy redactions that begin/end in the middle of a word, and some people are ok with it. You can't please everybody.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don"t get it does anyone mind explaining it to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You turned "The Knowledge" into an SCP, and did a wonderful job!

I'm starting to dig your work more and more, and considering I liked it from the start…

Is it weird that I actually like the Foundation publications? It makes it feel more like a real organization, to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Easily the best reason for an SCP to wind up in the Foundation's hands that I can recall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the first 'are' should be removed. You're on the right track!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, again, I'm not a native English speaker, so… can someone tell me if this line IS missing something or I'm the one not getting it?

Any disturbances or changes are noted in SCP-2830-21 are to be reported to Regional Command

I think it should be along the lines of 'If any disturbances', or maybe 'they are to be reported'?… oh, or 'changes that are noted'!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too am becoming less and less a fan of the internal Foundation references. I really think you overdid the footnotes, though I have to say #2 is the absolute clearest item description I have ever seen. Overall, I like it, regardless.

One thing I have to wonder: have they given a GPS to any of the 3-Positive subjects to determine if -2-Xi exists in our dimension? (I also have to wonder how one drives a cab while in that space.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn this is cool. I mean this is really cool!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The visual is helpful, but seems a bit unprofessional. It seems like something you'd see in a Powerpoint based on the SCP, but not something you'd see in the document itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot! I don't know that the PowerPoint-style slide was really necessary, and it actually threw me off for a bit there. Still, as a reason for existing, and I love Penrose's motivation in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you again! And yeah, it's not DNM but it's very much breathing the same air. Ways/magic/the Everything-Under-Everything is a more universal concept than the Foundation really has a full grasp of.

And I've added a new visual since your comment. Because the Foundation Database is a living whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's nice to see a reference to Foundation facilities located in a place that isn't North America. I feel like we don't see nearly enough of that.

Also, it might not be a Dixieland Nightmare Magic SCP, but…

The Everything-Under-Everything

Hm! Is that worldbuilding I see?

Fantastic writing, and fantastic visuals, as usual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw. No DNM? :(

Penrose's note makes me think of Pangloss and his notes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complaint is that I'm not a fan of the references to internal Foundation publications. However, that is a personal peeve, and it will not stop me from upvoting an article I enjoyed reading,

So, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just a well done SCP. You created a whole tiny universe and the story it contains, tidily wrapping it up from start to finish in a satisfying way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Then you will walk on your ᴡᴀʏ securely, and your foot will not stumble." - Proberbs 3:23 (ESV)

One of those myriad things we only find out about after it's over. In its day, the Knowledge roved the streets and understreets of London, providing the powerful a new class of transport, comfort and service. Penrose and his associates knew how to do one more thing well, too: the Knowledge finished on top. Life goes on in London, life goes on for Penrose (&c.), and life goes on for the Foundation.1

Footnotes
1. Photo credits: Gareth Wilcock (CC-BY), Erik Mauer (CC-BY-NC-SA), maps by Stamen (CC-BY). All other images by me or public domain.
